title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
The Property & Casualty (P&C) insurance industry has long struggled withunderperforming portfolios where the root causes of poor profitability and high volatility remain unknown. Traditional approaches to data-driven decision-making in insurance have largely focused on dashboard reporting, which is limited by the general lack of advanced analytical capabilities which offers a more powerful alternative. By combining data science, risk knowledge and industry expertise, insurers can generate actionable insights to grow, optimise existing portfolios and improve operational efficiency. As noted bythe Swiss Re Institute (2019), leveraging visual analytics can potentially help investigate trends in underlying loss drivers, while data enrichment using external data can help refine segmentation and underwriting strategy.
In this study, a motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset from a Spanish insurance company, made publicly available via Mendeley Data, is used to demonstrate the potential of visual analytics techniques in exploring and analysing key factors influencing portfolio profitability and volatility.



The data for this exercise is the motor vehicle insurance portfolio dataset retrieved from Mendeley Data.
The dataset contains 354,140 policy records across 47 variables, covering the period from 2022 to 2024. Variables are grouped into four broad categories:







Category
Description




Policy & Insured characteristics
Policy type, status, business origin, payment frequency, bonus score


Driver & Vehicle characteristics
Driver age, vehicle age, fuel type, vehicle brand, vehicle value, area


Premiums
Total and coverage-level premiums collected


Exposure, Claims & Incurred
Claims counts, incurred costs, and exposure duration






Using appropriate Exploratory Data Analysis (EDA) methods and ggplot2 functions, this study aims to:

Apply suitable univariate visual analytics techniques to explore the distributions and characteristics of numerical and categorical variables.
Apply suitable bivariate visual analytics techniques to investigate and statistically validate factors influencing the profitability and volatility of the insurance portfolio.

The code chunk below uses select() of the dplyr package to retain only the variables relevant to this study.

insurance <- insurance %>%
  select(insured_id, year,
         
         # Policy characteristics
         policy_type, policy_status, business_type,
         payment_frequency, bonus_score,
         
         # Driver & vehicle characteristics
         driver_age, vehicle_age, age_driving_licence,
         fuel_type, vehicle_value, seats,
         power_to_weight_ratio, vehicle_brand,
         municipality_type, circulation_area,
         
         # Premiums

         property_damage_premium, theft_premium,
         fire_premium, glass_premium,
         legal_protection_premium, occupants_premium,
         
         # Exposure, claims & incurred
         total_exposure, liability_exposure,
         total_claims, total_incurred)

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
4.3 Recoding Categorical Variables
recode() is primarily used to replace specific values in a vector with new ones. The recoded variable is then assigned using mutate() of the dplyr package.

Policy TypePolicy StatusBusiness TypePayment FrequencyBonus ScoreFuel TypeMunicipality TypeCirculation Area


policy_type is recoded to reflect the full coverage description:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_type = recode(policy_type,
    "TP"     = "Third-Party",
    "TPG"    = "Third-Party with Glass",
    "CC"     = "Third-Party Combined",
    "COMP_E" = "Comprehensive with Excess",
    "COMP_N" = "Comprehensive without Excess"))



policy_status is recoded to reflect whether the policy is active or cancelled:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_status = recode(policy_status,
    "A" = "Active",
    "C" = "Cancelled"))



business_type is recoded to reflect the business origin:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(business_type = recode(business_type,
    "NB" = "New Business",
    "P"  = "Portfolio"))



payment_frequency is recoded to reflect the billing frequency:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(payment_frequency = recode(payment_frequency,
    "A" = "Annual",
    "S" = "Semi-Annual",
    "Q" = "Quarterly"))



bonus_score is recoded to reflect the claims history:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(bonus_score = recode(bonus_score,
    "G" = "Good",
    "N" = "Neutral",
    "B" = "Bad"))



fuel_type is recoded to reflect the vehicle fuel type:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(fuel_type = recode(fuel_type,
    "D" = "Diesel",
    "G" = "Gasoline"))



municipality_type is recoded to reflect the geographic classification:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(municipality_type = recode(municipality_type,
    "I"  = "Inland",
    "C"  = "Coastal",
    "IS" = "Islands"))



circulation_area is recoded to reflect the circulation environment:

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(circulation_area = recode(circulation_area,
    "U" = "Urban",
    "R" = "Rural"))

4.4 Data Type Conversion
From the glimpse() output earlier, the categorical variables are currently stored as character (chr) data type. They need to be converted to factors (fct) so that R treats them correctly in visualisations and statistical analyses. mutate() and as.factor() of the dplyr package are used to perform the conversion.

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(policy_type       = as.factor(policy_type),
         policy_status     = as.factor(policy_status),
         business_type     = as.factor(business_type),
         payment_frequency = as.factor(payment_frequency),
         bonus_score       = as.factor(bonus_score),
         fuel_type         = as.factor(fuel_type),
         vehicle_brand     = as.factor(vehicle_brand),
         municipality_type = as.factor(municipality_type),
         circulation_area  = as.factor(circulation_area),
         year              = as.factor(year),
         seats             = as.factor(seats))

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
4.5 Variable Creation
Three new variables are created to support the analysis of portfolio profitability and volatility. mutate() of the dplyr package is used to create these variables.

4.5.1 Loss Ratio
loss_ratio is calculated as the ratio of total incurred costs to total premium collected. A loss ratio greater than 1 indicates that the insurer paid out more in claims than it collected in premiums, reflecting poor profitability.

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(loss_ratio = total_incurred / total_premium)



4.5.2 Claim Frequency
claim_frequency is calculated as the ratio of total claims to total exposure. It measures how often claims occur per policy year, reflecting the volatility of the portfolio.

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(claim_frequency = total_claims / total_exposure)



4.5.3 Has Claim
has_claim is a binary indicator variable that takes the value 1 if a policy has at least one claim, and 0 otherwise. It is useful for identifying which policies have experienced a loss.

insurance <- insurance %>%
  mutate(has_claim = ifelse(total_claims > 0, 1, 0))

The .rds file format is usually smaller than its CSV counterpart and will therefore take up less storage space. The .rds file will also preserve data types and classes such as factors and numeric variables, eliminating the need to redefine data types after loading the file. For fast and space efficient data storage, the cleaned dataset is exported as an RDS file using write_rds() and can be re-imported using read_rds().

write_rds(insurance, "insurance_clean.rds")


insurance <- read_rds("insurance_clean.rds")

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
5.1 Target Variables
The target variables are loss_ratio, claim_frequency, total_incurred and has_claim, which measure portfolio profitability, volatility, claim severity and claim occurrence respectively. As some variables contain extreme outliers, the analysis is restricted to a reasonable range for visualisation purposes.Before diving into the Exploratory Data Analysis, the distributions of key variables are examined individually to better understand their characteristics and identify any notable patterns.

Loss RatioClaim Frequency


loss_ratio measures the ratio of total incurred costs to total premium collected. A loss ratio greater than 1 indicates that the insurer paid out more in claims than it collected in premiums.
The raincloud plot below combines a half-violin plot, boxplot and individual data points to provide a comprehensive view of the distribution of loss_ratio.

insurance_lr <- insurance %>%
  filter(loss_ratio < 10,
         loss_ratio > 0)

ggplot(insurance_lr,
       aes(x = 0, y = loss_ratio)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill = "#4a7c6f") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2,
               outlier.shape = NA,
               fill = "#7aadab",
               alpha = 0.5) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio",
       subtitle = "Policies with loss ratio between 0 and 10",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Loss Ratio") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank())








The raincloud plot reveals that loss_ratio is highly right-skewed among policies with claims. Most policies record a loss ratio between 1.0 and 2.5, indicating that incurred costs typically exceed premiums collected. The long right tail suggests that a small number of policies experience exceptionally high losses, which significantly impacts overall portfolio profitability.
The histogram and boxplot below provide a complementary view of the distribution of loss_ratio.

p1 <- ggplot(insurance_lr,
             aes(x = loss_ratio)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.1,
                 fill = "#4a7c6f",
                 colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(loss_ratio)),
             colour = "red",
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(loss_ratio)),
             colour = "blue",
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = mean(insurance_lr$loss_ratio) + 0.3,
           y = Inf, vjust = 2,
           label = paste("Mean:", round(mean(insurance_lr$loss_ratio), 2)),
           colour = "red", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = median(insurance_lr$loss_ratio) + 0.3,
           y = Inf, vjust = 4,
           label = paste("Median:", round(median(insurance_lr$loss_ratio), 2)),
           colour = "blue", size = 3) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio",
       x = "Loss Ratio",
       y = "Count") +
  theme_minimal()

p2 <- ggplot(insurance_lr,
             aes(x = loss_ratio,
                 y = "")) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#7aadab",
               outlier.alpha = 0.3,
               outlier.size = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Loss Ratio",
       y = NULL) +
  theme_minimal()

p2 / p1 +
  plot_layout(heights = c(1, 3.5))








The distribution of loss_ratio is highly right-skewed, with the majority of policies having a loss ratio of 0 (no claims made). The mean loss ratio is higher than the median, indicating the presence of a small number of policies with very high loss ratios that pull the mean upward.


claim_frequency measures the ratio of total claims to total exposure. A higher claim frequency indicates greater volatility in the portfolio.
The raincloud plot below provides a comprehensive view of the distribution of claim_frequency.

insurance_cf <- insurance %>%
  filter(claim_frequency < 10,
         claim_frequency > 0)

ggplot(insurance_cf,
       aes(x = 0, y = claim_frequency)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA,
               fill = "#4a7c6f") +
  geom_boxplot(width = 0.2,
               outlier.shape = NA,
               fill = "#7aadab",
               alpha = 0.5) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.1,
            binwidth = 0.05,
            colour = "#4a7c6f",
            alpha = 0.3) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Claim Frequency",
       subtitle = "Policies with claim frequency between 0 and 10",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Claim Frequency") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.y = element_blank(),
        axis.ticks.y = element_blank())








The histogram and boxplot below provide a complementary view of the distribution of claim_frequency.

p3 <- ggplot(insurance_cf,
             aes(x = claim_frequency)) +
  geom_histogram(binwidth = 0.1,
                 fill = "#4a7c6f",
                 colour = "white") +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = mean(claim_frequency)),
             colour = "red",
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_vline(aes(xintercept = median(claim_frequency)),
             colour = "blue",
             linetype = "dashed",
             linewidth = 0.8) +
  annotate("text", x = mean(insurance_cf$claim_frequency) + 0.3,
           y = Inf, vjust = 2,
           label = paste("Mean:", round(mean(insurance_cf$claim_frequency), 2)),
           colour = "red", size = 3) +
  annotate("text", x = median(insurance_cf$claim_frequency) + 0.3,
           y = Inf, vjust = 4,

       x = "Claim Frequency",
       y = "Count") +

p4 <- ggplot(insurance_cf,
             aes(x = claim_frequency,
                 y = "")) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "#7aadab",
               outlier.alpha = 0.3,
               outlier.size = 0.5) +

p4 / p3 +

The distribution of claim_frequency is heavily right-skewed, with most policies recording a claim frequency of around 1, meaning one claim per policy year. The mean of 1.95 is noticeably higher than the median of 1.28, driven by a small number of high-frequency claimants. The boxplot reveals numerous outliers beyond the upper whisker, indicating that a subset of policies experience a disproportionately high number of claims, contributing significantly to portfolio volatility.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
Has Claim
has_claim is a binary variable indicating whether a policy has recorded at least one claim (1) or no claims (0). A bar chart is used to visualise the proportion of policies with and without claims.

insurance %>%
  mutate(has_claim = ifelse(has_claim == 1, 
                            "Has Claim", 
                            "No Claim")) %>%
  count(has_claim) %>%
  mutate(pct = n / sum(n) * 100) %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = has_claim,
             y = n,
             fill = has_claim)) +
  geom_col() +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(round(pct, 1), "%")),
            vjust = -0.5,
            size = 4) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Has Claim" = "#4a7c6f",
                                "No Claim" = "#7aadab")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Claim Occurrence",
       x = NULL,
       y = "Number of Policies") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")








The bar chart shows that 85.7% of policies in the portfolio have not recorded any claims, while only 14.3% have experienced at least one claim. This is typical of motor insurance portfolios where most policyholders do not make a claim in a given year. Despite being a small proportion, the 14.3% of policies with claims are of particular interest as they are the primary drivers of portfolio profitability and volatility.

text:

The distribution of driver_age is approximately bell-shaped and slightly right-skewed, with most drivers aged between 35 and 65 years. The mean age of 49 and median of 48 are very close, confirming a relatively symmetric distribution. The boxplot shows that the middle 50% of drivers fall between approximately 39 and 58 years old, with a small number of older drivers above 75 appearing as outliers.


bonus_score reflects the policyholder’s past claims experience — Good (G), Neutral (N) or Bad (B). A bar chart is used to visualise the proportion of policies in each category.

insurance %>%

The bar chart shows that 96.5% of policies belong to policyholders with a Good bonus score, indicating a strong claims history across the portfolio. Only 2.2% are Neutral and 1.3% are Bad. This suggests that the portfolio is dominated by older vehicles, which may reflect the nature of the Spanish non-life insurance market. The boxplot reveals a small number of very old vehicles (over 55 years) appearing as outliers.

insurance %>%

vehicle_brand represents the brand of the insured vehicle. As there are 65 brands in the dataset, only the top 10 most common brands are shown for clarity.

insurance %>%

text:
5.4 Policy Characteristics
This section explores the distribution of policy-related variables in the portfolio, specifically policy_type, policy_status, business_type and payment_frequency.

Policy TypePolicy StatusBusiness TypePayment Frequency

The bar chart shows that Third-Party Combined policies account for the largest share of the portfolio at 58%, followed by Comprehensive with Excess at 25.6%. Third-Party with Glass (10.2%) and Comprehensive without Excess (4.5%) make up smaller proportions, while basic Third-Party policies are the least common at only 1.6%. The dominance of Third-Party Combined policies suggests that most policyholders opt for broader coverage beyond basic liability, combining theft, fire and glass protection in a single policy.


policy_status indicates whether the policy was active or cancelled at the time of analysis. A bar chart is used to visualise the proportion of each policy status.

The bar chart shows that 89.2% of policies are Active, while 10.8% have been Cancelled. The low cancellation rate suggests a relatively stable and loyal customer base within the portfolio, with most policyholders choosing to maintain their coverage.


business_type classifies the policy according to its business origin either New Business (NB) or Portfolio (P). A bar chart is used to visualise the proportion of each business type.

The bar chart shows that 69.3% of policies are New Business, while 30.7% are Portfolio renewals. The higher proportion of new business suggests that the insurer has been actively growing its customer base during the period covered by the dataset, with roughly 7 out of 10 policies being newly acquired.


payment_frequency indicates how often the policy is billed — Annually, Semi-Annually or Quarterly. A bar chart is used to visualise the proportion of each payment frequency.

insurance %>%

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
6.1 Factors Affecting Loss Ratio (Profitability)
loss_ratio is used as the measure of portfolio profitability. Only policies with a loss ratio between 0 and 10 are included to exclude extreme outliers that may distort the analysis.

Bonus ScorePolicy TypeFuel TypeCirculation AreaDriver Age


The violin plot below examines whether bonus_score — reflecting a policyholder’s past claims history — has a significant effect on loss_ratio.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = bonus_score,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Bonus Score",
  xlab = "Bonus Score",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio across bonus score groups (p = 2.07e-19). Interestingly, policyholders with a Good bonus score have the highest median loss ratio of 2.02, followed by Neutral (1.60) and Bad (1.44). This counterintuitive finding may be attributed to the fact that Good bonus score policyholders dominate the portfolio (n = 35,663) and tend to hold more comprehensive policies, which attract higher claim costs. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant, confirming that bonus score is a meaningful differentiator of portfolio profitability.


The violin plot below examines whether policy_type has a significant effect on loss_ratio.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = policy_type,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Policy Type",
  xlab = "Policy Type",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio across policy types (p = 5.09e-92). The Third-Party policy type has the highest median loss ratio of 4.29 (n = 241), which is notably higher than all other policy types. This may be due to the very small sample size of Third-Party policies, where a few large claims can heavily skew the median upward.
Among the remaining policy types, Third-Party Combined (2.25) and Third-Party with Glass (2.22) have higher median loss ratios compared to Comprehensive with Excess (1.74) and Comprehensive without Excess (1.78). This suggests that comprehensive policies, despite offering broader coverage, are associated with lower loss ratios possibly because comprehensive policyholders tend to be more careful drivers who pay higher premiums, resulting in better profitability for the insurer.


The violin plot below examines whether fuel_type has a significant effect on loss_ratio.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = fuel_type,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Fuel Type",
  xlab = "Fuel Type",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The plot examines whether diesel or gasoline vehicles generate different loss ratios.
The Mann-Whitney test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio between fuel types (p = 3.48e-26). Gasoline vehicles have a higher median loss ratio of 2.22 (n = 10,902) compared to Diesel vehicles at 1.92 (n = 26,639). Although the effect size is small (r = -0.07), the difference is statistically significant, suggesting that gasoline vehicles tend to generate slightly less profitable policies than diesel vehicles. This could be attributed to differences in driving behaviour or vehicle characteristics between gasoline and diesel vehicle owners.


The violin plot below examines whether circulation_area has a significant effect on loss_ratio.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = circulation_area,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Circulation Area",
  xlab = "Circulation Area",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Mann-Whitney test confirms a statistically significant difference in loss ratio between circulation areas (p = 2.68e-07). Surprisingly, Rural drivers have a slightly higher median loss ratio of 2.06 (n = 15,954) compared to Urban drivers at 1.95 (n = 21,587). However, the effect size is very small (r = 0.03), suggesting that while the difference is statistically significant, it is not practically meaningful. This indicates that circulation area alone may not be a strong predictor of portfolio profitability.


The scatterplot below examines whether driver_age has a significant relationship with loss_ratio.

ggscatterstats(
  data = insurance_lr,
  x    = driver_age,
  y    = loss_ratio,
  type = "nonparametric",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Loss Ratio by Driver Age",
  xlab = "Driver Age (years)",
  ylab = "Loss Ratio",
  marginal = TRUE
)








The Spearman correlation test reveals a statistically significant but very weak positive relationship between driver age and loss ratio (ρ = 0.03, p = 1.00e-07). The nearly flat blue trend line confirms that while older drivers tend to have a very slightly higher loss ratio, the relationship is negligible in practical terms. The histograms show that most drivers are aged between 30 and 70, while most loss ratios are concentrated below 2.5. Overall, driver age alone is not a strong predictor of portfolio profitability.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
6.2 Factors Affecting Claim Frequency (Volatility)
claim_frequency is used as the measure of portfolio volatility. Only policies with a claim frequency between 0 and 10 are included to exclude extreme outliers.

Bonus ScorePolicy TypeMunicipality TypeCirculation AreaDriver Age


The violin plot below examines whether bonus_score has a significant effect on claim_frequency.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_cf,
  x    = bonus_score,
  y    = claim_frequency,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Claim Frequency by Bonus Score",
  xlab = "Bonus Score",
  ylab = "Claim Frequency",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in claim frequency across bonus score groups (p = 8.47e-61). As expected, policyholders with a Bad bonus score have the highest median claim frequency of 1.98 (n = 999), followed by Neutral (1.81, n = 1,333) and Good (1.26, n = 47,383). This pattern is consistent with expectations where policyholders with a poor claims history tend to make claims more frequently, contributing to greater portfolio volatility. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant, confirming that bonus score is a strong and meaningful predictor of claim frequency.


The violin plot below examines whether policy_type has a significant effect on claim_frequency.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_cf,
  x    = policy_type,
  y    = claim_frequency,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Claim Frequency by Policy Type",
  xlab = "Policy Type",
  ylab = "Claim Frequency",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in claim frequency across policy types (p = 3.28e-298). Comprehensive without Excess policies have the highest median claim frequency of 2.00 (n = 4,836), followed by Comprehensive with Excess (1.40, n = 16,470). In contrast, Third-Party based policies have notably lower claim frequencies — Third-Party Combined (1.17, n = 24,157), Third-Party with Glass (1.18, n = 3,868) and Third-Party (1.22, n = 384). This suggests that comprehensive policyholders tend to make claims more frequently, possibly because broader coverage lowers the threshold for filing a claim. All pairwise comparisons are statistically significant.


The violin plot below examines whether municipality_type has a significant effect on claim_frequency.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_cf,
  x    = municipality_type,
  y    = claim_frequency,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Claim Frequency by Municipality Type",
  xlab = "Municipality Type",
  ylab = "Claim Frequency",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Kruskal-Wallis test confirms a statistically significant difference in claim frequency across municipality types (p = 6.41e-05). However the differences are very small, Inland municipalities have the highest median claim frequency of 1.29 (n = 33,163), closely followed by Coastal (1.28, n = 13,502) and Islands (1.17, n = 3,050). The effect size is negligible, suggesting that while statistically significant, municipality type has minimal practical impact on claim frequency.


The violin plot below examines whether circulation_area has a significant effect on claim_frequency.

ggbetweenstats(
  data = insurance_cf,
  x    = circulation_area,
  y    = claim_frequency,
  type = "nonparametric",
  mean.ci = TRUE,
  pairwise.comparisons = TRUE,
  pairwise.display = "significant",
  p.adjust.method = "fdr",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Claim Frequency by Circulation Area",
  xlab = "Circulation Area",
  ylab = "Claim Frequency",
  palette = "Set2"
)








The Mann-Whitney test confirms a statistically significant difference in claim frequency between circulation areas (p = 4.86e-13). Urban drivers have a higher median claim frequency of 1.33 (n = 28,271) compared to Rural drivers at 1.23 (n = 21,444). This aligns with expectations where urban environments with higher traffic density tend to result in more frequent claims. However, the effect size is small (r = -0.04), indicating that while the difference is statistically significant, circulation area has limited practical impact on portfolio volatility.


The scatterplot below examines whether driver_age has a significant relationship with claim_frequency.

ggscatterstats(
  data = insurance_cf,
  x    = driver_age,
  y    = claim_frequency,
  type = "nonparametric",
  messages = FALSE,
  title = "Claim Frequency by Driver Age",
  xlab = "Driver Age (years)",
  ylab = "Claim Frequency",
  marginal = TRUE
)








The Spearman correlation test reveals a statistically significant but very weak negative relationship between driver age and claim frequency (ρ = -0.04, p = 3.07e-20). The nearly flat blue trend line confirms that older drivers tend to have a very slightly lower claim frequency, contrary to the common assumption that older drivers are higher risk. The effect size is negligible, suggesting that driver age alone is not a meaningful predictor of portfolio volatility in this dataset.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
6.3 Distribution Comparison across Key Groups
To complement the statistical analysis in Sections 6.1 and 6.2, ridgeline plots are used to compare the full distribution of loss_ratio and claim_frequency across key grouping variables. Ridgeline plots are particularly useful for visualising how distributions shift across multiple groups simultaneously.

Loss Ratio by Policy TypeClaim Frequency by Bonus Score




Code
insurance_lr %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = loss_ratio,
             y = policy_type,
             fill = policy_type)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7,
                      scale = 0.9,
                      quantile_lines = TRUE,
                      quantiles = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Comprehensive with Excess"    = "#4a7c6f",
    "Comprehensive without Excess" = "#7aadab",
    "Third-Party"                  = "#d4e8e1",
    "Third-Party Combined"         = "#2d5a51",
    "Third-Party with Glass"       = "#a8d5c2")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Loss Ratio by Policy Type",
       subtitle = "Vertical line shows median",
       x = "Loss Ratio",
       y = "Policy Type") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









The ridgeline plot provides a clearer view of how loss ratio distributions differ across policy types. Comprehensive with Excess and Third-Party Combined show the narrowest and most concentrated distributions, peaking sharply around 1–2, indicating consistent and predictable loss behaviour. Comprehensive without Excess and Third-Party with Glass display slightly wider distributions with longer right tails, suggesting greater variability. Most notably, Third-Party policies exhibit a distinctive bimodal distribution with two peaks, one around 1–2 and another around 4–5, suggesting two distinct subgroups with very different loss behaviours. This may be attributed to its small sample size (n = 241) where a few extreme cases disproportionately influence the distribution shape.
Overall, the ridgeline plot reinforces that Comprehensive policies generate more stable and predictable loss ratios, while Third-Party policies, particularly basic Third-Party exhibit greater variability and higher losses.




Code
insurance_cf %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = claim_frequency,
             y = bonus_score,
             fill = bonus_score)) +
  geom_density_ridges(alpha = 0.7,
                      scale = 0.9,
                      quantile_lines = TRUE,
                      quantiles = 2) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c(
    "Good"    = "#4a7c6f",
    "Neutral" = "#7aadab",
    "Bad"     = "#d4e8e1")) +
  labs(title = "Distribution of Claim Frequency by Bonus Score",
       subtitle = "Vertical line shows median",
       x = "Claim Frequency",
       y = "Bonus Score") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "none")









The ridgeline plot clearly shows distinct distribution shapes across bonus score groups. Good bonus score policies show a sharp, concentrated peak around 1, reflecting consistently low claim frequencies among the majority of the portfolio. Neutral policies display a wider bimodal distribution with two visible peaks, suggesting greater variability in claim behaviour.
Bad bonus score policies (median = 1.90) show the widest and most right-skewed distribution, with a long tail extending beyond 6, visually confirming the statistical findings from Section 6.2 that policyholders with a poor claims history not only make more frequent claims but also exhibit the most unpredictable claim behaviour in the portfolio.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
Key Findings
Portfolio Overview
The portfolio is dominated by middle-aged drivers holding Third-Party Combined policies, with the vast majority of policyholders recording no claims. This suggests a generally low-risk customer base, with a small but significant minority of high-risk policyholders driving most of the portfolio’s losses.
Profitability (Loss Ratio)
Policy type emerges as the strongest differentiator of portfolio profitability. Basic Third-Party policies are the least profitable despite being the least common, while Comprehensive with Excess policies generate the most consistent and stable loss ratios. This suggests that broader coverage does not necessarily translate to higher losses, in fact, comprehensive policyholders tend to be more careful drivers who pay higher premiums.
Fuel type and circulation area, while statistically significant, have negligible practical impact on profitability and are unlikely to be meaningful underwriting factors on their own.
Volatility (Claim Frequency)
Bonus score is the most powerful predictor of claim frequency. Policyholders with a Bad bonus score reflects a history of past claims, make significantly more frequent claims, contributing disproportionately to portfolio volatility. This confirms that past claims history is a reliable indicator of future claim behaviour.
Comprehensive without Excess policies stand out as the most volatile policy type, likely because the absence of an excess removes the financial disincentive to file small claims. In contrast, Third-Party Combined policies show the most stable and predictable claim behaviour.

title: Take-home Exercise 1: Uncovering Loss Drivers in Motor Vehicle Insurance Portfolio using Data Visualisation
text:
Recommendations for Future Research

Confirmatory Data Analysis — Future studies should incorporate additional statistical tests such as regression modelling to validate the findings from this exploratory analysis and establish more definitive relationships between the identified risk factors and portfolio outcomes.
Exploring Additional Variables — A more exhaustive analysis incorporating a wider range of variables could yield deeper insights into portfolio profitability and volatility. Variables such as driver occupation, annual mileage, claims history duration and vehicle usage type could provide a more complete picture of risk segmentation.
Longitudinal Analysis — Conducting longitudinal studies to track changes in loss ratios and claim frequencies over a longer time period can reveal trends and seasonal patterns that a three-year snapshot may not capture.
Incorporating External Data — As highlighted by the Swiss Re Institute (2019), enriching the portfolio data with external sources such as traffic density, weather patterns and regional accident statistics could further refine segmentation and improve underwriting strategy.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
section: Dashboard 1 — English Score Distribution by Class
text:
Dashboard 1 — English Score Distribution by Class
This dashboard shows a boxplot of English scores by class alongside a histogram of the overall score distribution. The average English score across all students is 67.18.



English Score Distribution by Class


From the Exam Data, I noticed that 3D performed better than 3C in the English exam, as 3D has a higher overall score distribution. This shows that the boxplot is useful because it allows me to compare not only the general performance between classes, but also the spread and consistency of scores within each class.
The boxplot also helps reduce the zone of irrelevancy. In a bar chart, the viewer may focus too much on small differences between bars that may not be meaningful, making minor gaps look more important than they really are. With a boxplot, I can see whether the scores actually overlap, whether the difference between classes is meaningful, and whether the overall distribution supports the conclusion. This makes the boxplot more useful for comparing exam performance across classes.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text:
Dashboard 2 — Food Service KPI Report
This dashboard uses diverging stacked bar charts to compare food service satisfaction ratings across different wards and months (October and November).



Food Service KPI Report


For the Food Service Survey Data, the dashboards help summarise many responses in a clear and organised way. The charts made it easier to compare satisfaction levels across different food service items, months and wards. I could quickly see which areas received more positive ratings and which areas may need improvement.

title: In-Class Exercise 4: Visual Statistical Analysis with Tableau
text:
Reflections
Overall, I learnt that visual analytics is useful because it turns raw data into insights that are easier to understand, explain and use for decision making. Different chart types serve different purposes — boxplots are better for comparing distributions across groups, while diverging stacked bar charts work well for summarising Likert-scale survey data across multiple categories.

text: Parallel coordinates plots are designed for comparing multiple numerical variables across many observations simultaneously. In this exercise I used the World Happiness 2018 data to explore patterns across happiness indicators by region.

title: 5d. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Parallel Coordinates Plot
text:
Reflections
The main strength of parallel coordinates plots is that they can show many variables at once without needing separate charts. Scaling with uniminmax was important because without it, variables with different units and ranges are impossible to compare on the same plot. The facet version by region revealed clear differences in the happiness profiles of different parts of the world. The interactive parallelPlot version was more practical for analysis since dragging on any axis filters the lines and makes it easier to focus on specific subgroups.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text: A correlation matrix shows the pairwise correlation coefficients between all variables in a dataset. In this exercise I used the Wine Quality dataset to explore relationships between chemical properties of wine.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Reflections
There are several ways to visualise a correlation matrix in R, each suited for different purposes. pairs() gives a quick scatter plot overview, ggcorrmat() is useful because it layers in statistical significance automatically, and corrplot() is the most visually polished and flexible. Reordering by hierarchical clustering was a useful technique, it groups related variables together, making underlying patterns much easier to read. The significance test overlay also helped distinguish meaningful correlations from noise.

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(corrplot, ggstatsplot, tidyverse)



Importing data

wine <- read_csv("wine_quality.csv")

title: 5b. Visual Correlation Analysis
text:
Building Correlation Matrix — pairs() method

Basic correlation matrix

The plotThe code chunk














pairs(wine[,1:11])






Lower corner only

The plotThe code chunk














pairs(wine[,2:12], upper.panel = NULL)






Upper corner only

The plotThe code chunk














pairs(wine[,2:12], lower.panel = NULL)






With correlation coefficients

The plotThe code chunk














panel.cor <- function(x, y, digits=2, prefix="", cex.cor, ...) {
  usr <- par("usr")
  on.exit(par(usr))
  par(usr = c(0, 1, 0, 1))
  r <- abs(cor(x, y, use="complete.obs"))
  txt <- format(c(r, 0.123456789), digits=digits)[1]
  txt <- paste(prefix, txt, sep="")
  if(missing(cex.cor)) cex.cor <- 0.8/strwidth(txt)
  text(0.5, 0.5, txt, cex = cex.cor * (1 + r) / 2)
}
pairs(wine[,2:12], upper.panel = panel.cor)

         tl.srt = 45,

               lower = "ellipse",

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text: A funnel plot is used for fair comparison between groups of different sizes. In this exercise I used COVID-19 data from Jakarta to compare cumulative fatality rates across sub-districts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text: A funnel plot is used for fair comparison between groups of different sizes. In this exercise I used COVID-19 data from Jakarta to compare cumulative fatality rates across sub-districts.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Reflections
Funnel plots were completely new to me. The key insight is that comparing raw rates between areas of different sizes is unfair, a small sub-district with 2 deaths out of 10 cases looks much worse than a large one with 200 deaths out of 5000 cases, even if the true rate is similar.
The funnel shape comes from the fact that uncertainty decreases as sample size grows, small areas sit further out in the funnel, and outliers outside the 95% or 99% limits are the ones worth investigating.
Building the ggplot2 version from scratch was challenging but helped me understand exactly how the confidence limits are calculated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(tidyverse, FunnelPlotR, plotly, knitr)



Importing data

  mutate_if(is.character, as.factor)

Plot is adjusted for overdispersion.

  x_label = "Cumulative COVID-19 Positive Cases",
  y_label = "Cumulative Fatality Rate"

title: Hands-on Exercise 4d: Building Funnel Plot with R
text:
Funnel Plot using ggplot2

Computing derived fields

df <- covid19 %>%
  mutate(rate = Death / Positive) %>%
  mutate(rate.se = sqrt((rate*(1-rate)) / (Positive))) %>%
  filter(rate > 0)

fit.mean <- weighted.mean(df$rate, 1/df$rate.se^2)



Calculate 95% and 99.9% confidence limits

number.seq <- seq(1, max(df$Positive), 1)
number.ll95 <- fit.mean - 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ul95 <- fit.mean + 1.96 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ll999 <- fit.mean - 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
number.ul999 <- fit.mean + 3.29 * sqrt((fit.mean*(1-fit.mean)) / (number.seq))
dfCI <- data.frame(number.ll95, number.ul95,
                   number.ll999, number.ul999,
                   number.seq, fit.mean)



Static funnel plot

The plotThe code chunk














p <- ggplot(df, aes(x = Positive, y = rate)) +
  geom_point(aes(label=`Sub-district`),
             alpha=0.4,
             size=0.9) +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ll95),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40",
            linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,
                y = number.ul95),
            linewidth = 0.4,
            colour = "grey40",
            linetype = "dashed") +
  geom_line(data = dfCI,
            aes(x = number.seq,

title: Hands-on Exercise 4b: Visual Statistical Analysis
text:
ggstatsplot is really useful because it combines the chart and the statistical test result in one — something that would normally take two separate steps in R. The ggbetweenstats() function was the most interesting since it automatically runs the right test depending on the number of groups and shows the pairwise comparisons directly on the chart.
The Bayes Factor in gghistostats() was new to me — it gives a more intuitive way to interpret evidence compared to just a p-value.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated graphics can make data stories more engaging and memorable compared to static charts. In this exercise, I learnt how to create animated visualisations using gganimate and plotly, while also practising data wrangling with tidyr and dplyr.
Before diving in, it’s worth asking, does animation actually add value here? For exploratory analysis, probably not. But for presentations, a well-placed animation can really help the audience connect with the data.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Reflections
This was my first time making charts interactive in R. The ggiraph package was surprisingly straightforward — just swap geom_point() for geom_point_interactive() and wrap it in girafe().
The most interesting part was the coordinated multiple views where hovering on one chart highlights the same student in another chart. This is really useful for spotting patterns across different variables at the same time.
I found plotly easier to use than ggiraph since ggplotly() can just wrap an existing ggplot chart. The crosstalk + DT combination was also very cool, being able to filter a chart by clicking on a table row is something I can see being very useful in real dashboards.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(readxl, gifski, gapminder,
               plotly, gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing data
This exercise uses a different dataset — GlobalPopulation.xls instead of Exam_data.csv.

col <- c("Country", "Continent")
globalPop <- read_xls("GlobalPopulation.xls",
                      sheet="Data") %>%
  mutate(across(all_of(col), as.factor)) %>%
  mutate(Year = as.integer(Year))

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation — gganimate

Static bubble plot first
Always good to build the static version before animating — helps check the layout looks right.

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young')






Animated bubble plot
Adding transition_time() animates the chart across years. ease_aes('linear') makes the movement smooth and consistent.

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(globalPop, aes(x = Old, y = Young, 
                      size = Population, 
                      colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.7, 
             show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(title = 'Year: {frame_time}', 
       x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') +
  transition_time(Year) +       
  ease_aes('linear')

title: Hands-on Exercise 3b: Programming Animated Statistical Graphics with R
text:
Animated Data Visualisation — plotly

Using ggplotly()
This converts the ggplot animation into an interactive plotly chart with a play/pause button and a year slider — much more user-friendly for presentations.

The plotThe code chunk











gg <- ggplot(globalPop, 
       aes(x = Old, 
           y = Young, 
           size = Population, 
           colour = Country)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = Population,
                 frame = Year),
             alpha = 0.7) +
  scale_colour_manual(values = country_colors) +
  scale_size(range = c(2, 12)) +
  labs(x = '% Aged', 
       y = '% Young') + 
  theme(legend.position='none')

ggplotly(gg)






Using plot_ly()
Building the animated chart directly with plot_ly() — slightly different syntax but gives more control over the output.

The plotThe code chunk











bp <- globalPop %>%
  plot_ly(x = ~Old, 
          y = ~Young, 
          size = ~Population, 
          color = ~Continent,
          sizes = c(2, 100),
          frame = ~Year, 
          text = ~Country, 
          hoverinfo = "text",
          type = 'scatter',
          mode = 'markers') %>%
  layout(showlegend = FALSE)
bp

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text: In this exercise, I explored ggplot2 extensions that go beyond basic charts. I learned how ggrepel prevents overlapping text labels, making scatter plots much cleaner and easier to read. I also experimented with ggthemes and hrbrthemes to apply professional styling, and used patchwork to combine multiple plots into a single layout. These tools showed me that good data visualisation is as much about presentation as it is about the data itself.

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond ggplot2 Annotation: ggrepel
One of the challenges when annotating scatter plots is that labels often overlap each other. The ggrepel package solves this by automatically adjusting label positions.

Without ggrepel (messy labels)

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label(aes(label = ID), 
             hjust = .5, 
             vjust = -.5) +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")






With ggrepel (clean labels)

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x= MATHS, 
           y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  geom_label_repel(aes(label = ID), 
                   fontface = "bold") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")

                 color="grey25",

Using hrbrthemes: theme_ipsum()

The plotThe code

       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_ipsum()






theme_ipsum() with custom settings

The plotThe code

       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25",

  theme_ipsum(axis_title_size = 18,

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Beyond Single Graph
The patchwork package makes it easy to combine multiple ggplot2 charts into one layout.

Creating the individual plots

p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores")

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, 
                 boundary = 100,
                 color="grey25", 
                 fill="grey90") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("Distribution of English scores")

p3 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
             aes(x= MATHS, 
                 y=ENGLISH)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method=lm, linewidth=0.5) +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100),
                  ylim=c(0,100)) +
  ggtitle("English scores versus Maths scores for Primary 3")



Combining two graphs

The plotThe code














p1 + p2






Combining three graphs

The plotThe code














(p1 / p2) | p3






With tags

The plotThe code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'I')






With inset

The plotThe code














p3 + inset_element(p2, 
                   left = 0.02, 
                   bottom = 0.7, 
                   right = 0.5, 
                   top = 1)






patchwork + ggtheme combined

The plotThe code














patchwork <- (p1 / p2) | p3
patchwork & theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 2: Beyond ggplot2 Fundamentals
text:
Extra Alternatives

Alternative 1: Using theme_wsj()
I used theme_wsj() instead of theme_economist() for a cleaner, more minimal Wall Street Journal style.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_wsj()






Alternative 2: Using theme_fivethirtyeight()
This theme mimics the style used by FiveThirtyEight, great for clean data journalism visuals.

The plotThe code














ggplot(data=exam_data, aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_histogram(bins=20, color="grey25", fill="grey90") +
  ggtitle("Distribution of Maths scores") +
  theme_fivethirtyeight()






Alternative 3: Different patchwork layout
Instead of stacking p1/p2 on the left, I place p1 on top spanning full width for better visual hierarchy.

The plotThe code














p1 / (p2 | p3)






Alternative 4: Letter tags instead of Roman numerals
Using letter tags (A, B, C) makes subplots easier to reference in written explanations.

The plotThe code














((p1 / p2) | p3) + 
  plot_annotation(tag_levels = 'A')






Alternative 5: Inset in different position
Placing the inset in the bottom right instead of top left avoids covering the main data cluster.

The plotThe code














p3 + inset_element(p2,
                   left = 0.5,
                   bottom = 0.0,
                   right = 1.0,
                   top = 0.4)

title: Hands-on Exercise 1: A Layered Grammar of Graphics
text: In this exercise, I was introduced to ggplot2 and the layered grammar of graphics. I learned that every chart in R is built by adding layers — starting from data, then aesthetics, then geometry. I explored many different chart types including histograms, dot plots, box plots, violin plots, and scatter plots. I also discovered how facets allow us to split charts by categories, and how built-in themes like theme_classic() and theme_minimal() can instantly change the look of a chart. This exercise showed me that ggplot2 is both systematic and flexible.

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this exercise, I explored how to make static ggplot2 charts interactive using the ggiraph and plotly packages. Key techniques include:

Adding tooltips to display information on hover
Using data_id to highlight related elements together
Creating onclick effects to link charts to external pages
Building coordinated multiple views where interactions on one chart affect another
Using DT to create searchable, interactive data tables
Using crosstalk to link a chart and a table together




Interactivity makes charts more useful for exploration — users can investigate specific data points without needing a separate table or annotation.




Understanding the difference between tooltip, data_id and onclick arguments in ggiraph
Setting up coordinated views required linking plots through a shared highlight_key()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
In this exercise, I explored how to make static ggplot2 charts interactive using the ggiraph and plotly packages. Key techniques include:

Adding tooltips to display information on hover
Using data_id to highlight related elements together
Creating onclick effects to link charts to external pages
Building coordinated multiple views where interactions on one chart affect another
Using DT to create searchable, interactive data tables
Using crosstalk to link a chart and a table together




Interactivity makes charts more useful for exploration — users can investigate specific data points without needing a separate table or annotation.




Understanding the difference between tooltip, data_id and onclick arguments in ggiraph
Setting up coordinated views required linking plots through a shared highlight_key()

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(ggiraph, plotly, 
               patchwork, DT, 
               crosstalk, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam_data <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 3a: Programming Interactive Data Visualisation with R
text:
Interactive Data Visualisation — ggiraph methods

Tooltip effect
Hovering over a data point shows the student’s ID.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = ID),
    stackgroups = TRUE, 
    binwidth = 1, 
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL, 
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 6,
  height_svg = 6*0.618
)






Displaying multiple information on tooltip
Hovering shows both the student’s ID and Class.

The plotThe code chunk











exam_data$tooltip <- c(paste0(     
  "Name = ", exam_data$ID,         
  "\n Class = ", exam_data$CLASS)) 

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(
    aes(tooltip = exam_data$tooltip), 
    stackgroups = TRUE,
    binwidth = 1,
    method = "histodot") +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(
  ggobj = p,
  width_svg = 8,
  height_svg = 8*0.618
)






Customising tooltip style
Using CSS to change tooltip background and font colour.

The plotThe code chunk











tooltip_css <- "background-color:white;
font-style:bold; color:black;"

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = ID),                   
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(
    opts_tooltip(
      css = tooltip_css))
)






Displaying statistics on tooltip
Tooltip shows the mean Maths score with confidence interval per race.

The plotThe code chunk











tooltip <- function(y, ymax, accuracy = .01) {
  mean <- scales::number(y, accuracy = accuracy)
  sem <- scales::number(ymax - y, accuracy = accuracy)
  paste("Mean maths scores:", mean, "+/-", sem)
}

gg_point <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
                   aes(x = RACE)) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS, 
                   tooltip = after_stat(  
                     tooltip(y, ymax))),  
    fun.data = "mean_se", 
    geom = GeomInteractiveCol,  
    fill = "light blue"
  ) +
  stat_summary(aes(y = MATHS),
    fun.data = mean_se,
    geom = "errorbar", width = 0.2, size = 0.2
  )

girafe(ggobj = gg_point,
       width_svg = 8,
       height_svg = 8*0.618)






Hover effect with data_id
Elements of the same CLASS are highlighted together on mouse over.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(           
    aes(data_id = CLASS),             
    stackgroups = TRUE,               
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618                      
)






Styling hover effect
Custom CSS changes the highlight colour and dims non-hovered elements.

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)






Combining tooltip and hover effect

The plotThe code chunk











p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(tooltip = CLASS, 
        data_id = CLASS),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618,
  options = list(                        
    opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),  
    opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;") 
  )                                        
)






Click effect with onclick
Clicking a data point opens the MOE school finder page.

The plotThe code chunk











exam_data$onclick <- sprintf("window.open(\"%s%s\")",
"https://www.moe.gov.sg/schoolfinder?journey=Primary%20school",
as.character(exam_data$ID))

p <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(onclick = onclick),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +               
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)
girafe(                                  
  ggobj = p,                             
  width_svg = 6,                         
  height_svg = 6*0.618)






Coordinated Multiple Views with ggiraph
Hovering highlights the same student across both plots simultaneously.

The plotThe code chunk











p1 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = MATHS)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") +  
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

p2 <- ggplot(data=exam_data, 
       aes(x = ENGLISH)) +
  geom_dotplot_interactive(              
    aes(data_id = ID),              
    stackgroups = TRUE,                  
    binwidth = 1,                        
    method = "histodot") + 
  coord_cartesian(xlim=c(0,100)) +
  scale_y_continuous(NULL,               
                     breaks = NULL)

girafe(code = print(p1 + p2), 
       width_svg = 6,
       height_svg = 3,
       options = list(
         opts_hover(css = "fill: #202020;"),
         opts_hover_inv(css = "opacity:0.2;")
         )
       )

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text: In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using ggridges and ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text: In this exercise I learnt two relatively new statistical graphic methods for visualising distribution — ridgeline plots and raincloud plots — using ggridges and ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Reflections
This exercise introduced two new ways to visualise distribution. Ridgeline plots are great for comparing distributions across many groups at once, much cleaner than stacking multiple histograms.
The raincloud plot was the most interesting, it combines three views in one chart: the density shape, the boxplot summary, and the actual data points. This gives a much more complete picture than just a boxplot alone.
The step-by-step build up of the raincloud plot (half eye → boxplot → dots → flip) was a good way to understand how each layer adds information.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(ggdist, ggridges, ggthemes,
               colorspace, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising Distribution with Ridgeline Plot
A ridgeline plot (also called a Joyplot) reveals the distribution of a numeric value for several groups. It works best when there are 5 or more groups with a clear pattern or ranking.

Basic ridgeline plot

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS)) +
  geom_density_ridges(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01,
    bandwidth = 3.4,
    fill = lighten("#7097BB", .3),
    color = "white"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
    ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






Varying fill colours along the x axis

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = stat(x))) +
  geom_density_ridges_gradient(
    scale = 3,
    rel_min_height = 0.01) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Temp. [F]",
                       option = "C") +
  scale_x_continuous(
    name = "English grades",
    expand = c(0, 0)
  ) +
  scale_y_discrete(name = NULL, expand = expansion(add = c(0.2, 2.6))) +
  theme_ridges()






Mapping probabilities directly onto colour

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = 0.5 - abs(0.5-stat(ecdf)))) +
  stat_density_ridges(geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
                      calc_ecdf = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_c(name = "Tail probability",
                       direction = -1) +
  theme_ridges()






Ridgeline plots with quantile lines

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = 4,
    quantile_lines = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_viridis_d(name = "Quartiles") +
  theme_ridges()






Ridgeline plot with tail probability cut points

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(exam,
       aes(x = ENGLISH,
           y = CLASS,
           fill = factor(stat(quantile))
           )) +
  stat_density_ridges(
    geom = "density_ridges_gradient",
    calc_ecdf = TRUE,
    quantiles = c(0.025, 0.975)
    ) +
  scale_fill_manual(
    name = "Probability",
    values = c("#FF0000A0", "#A0A0A0A0", "#0000FFA0"),
    labels = c("(0, 0.025]", "(0.025, 0.975]", "(0.975, 1]")
  ) +
  theme_ridges()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4a: Visualising Distribution
text:
Visualising Distribution with Raincloud Plot
A raincloud plot combines a half-density plot, a boxplot and a dot plot — giving a much richer picture of the data distribution than a boxplot alone.

Step 1 — Half Eye graph

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA)






Step 2 — Adding boxplot with geom_boxplot()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA)






Step 3 — Adding dot plots stat_dots()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2)






Step 4 — Final raincloud plot

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = ENGLISH)) +
  stat_halfeye(adjust = 0.5,
               justification = -0.2,
               .width = 0,
               point_colour = NA) +
  geom_boxplot(width = .20,
               outlier.shape = NA) +
  stat_dots(side = "left",
            justification = 1.2,
            binwidth = .5,
            dotsize = 2) +
  coord_flip() +
  theme_economist()

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text: Visualising uncertainty is about showing not just the point estimate (like a mean) but also how confident we are in that estimate. In this exercise I learnt to plot error bars, confidence intervals and uncertainty using ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text: Visualising uncertainty is about showing not just the point estimate (like a mean) but also how confident we are in that estimate. In this exercise I learnt to plot error bars, confidence intervals and uncertainty using ggdist.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Reflections
The difference between standard error bars and confidence intervals was much clearer after seeing them plotted side by side. The 95% CI version (mean ± 1.96 × SE) is more informative than just showing SE.
The interactive version combining plotly and DT was the most practical, being able to hover over a point and see the exact confidence interval values, and then filter the table at the same time, is very useful for presentations.
stat_gradientinterval() from ggdist was visually striking — the gradient effect gives a more intuitive feel for where the uncertainty is concentrated.

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, crosstalk, DT,
               ggdist, ggridges, colorspace,
               gganimate, tidyverse)



Importing data

exam <- read_csv("Exam_data.csv")



Computing summary statistics

my_sum <- exam %>%
  group_by(RACE) %>%
  summarise(
    n=n(),
    mean=mean(MATHS),
    sd=sd(MATHS)
    ) %>%
  mutate(se=sd/sqrt(n-1))

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Visualising Uncertainty — ggplot2 methods

Standard error bars

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=RACE,
        ymin=mean-se,
        ymax=mean+se),
    width=0.2,
    colour="black",
    alpha=0.9,
    linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat="identity",
             color="red",
             size = 1.5,
             alpha=1) +
  ggtitle("Standard error of mean maths score by race")






95% Confidence interval

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(my_sum) +
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
        ymin=mean-1.96*se,
        ymax=mean+1.96*se),
    width=0.2,
    colour="black",
    alpha=0.9,
    linewidth=0.5) +
  geom_point(aes(x=RACE,
                 y=mean),
             stat="identity",
             color="red",
             size = 1.5,
             alpha=1) +
  labs(x = "Maths score",
       title = "95% confidence interval of mean maths score by race")






Interactive error bars — 99% CI

The plotThe code chunk





















shared_df = SharedData$new(my_sum)

bscols(widths = c(4,8),
       ggplotly((ggplot(shared_df) +
                   geom_errorbar(aes(
                     x=reorder(RACE, -mean),
                     ymin=mean-2.58*se,
                     ymax=mean+2.58*se),
                     width=0.2,
                     colour="black",
                     alpha=0.9,
                     size=0.5) +
                   geom_point(aes(
                     x=RACE,
                     y=mean,
                     text = paste("Race:", `RACE`,
                                  "<br>N:", `n`,
                                  "<br>Avg. Scores:", round(mean, digits = 2),
                                  "<br>95% CI:[",
                                  round((mean-2.58*se), digits = 2), ",",
                                  round((mean+2.58*se), digits = 2),"]")),
                     stat="identity",
                     color="red",
                     size = 1.5,
                     alpha=1) +
                   xlab("Race") +
                   ylab("Average Scores") +
                   theme_minimal() +
                   theme(axis.text.x = element_text(
                     angle = 45, vjust = 0.5, hjust=1)) +
                   ggtitle("99% Confidence interval of average maths scores by race")),
                tooltip = "text"),
       DT::datatable(shared_df,
                     rownames = FALSE,
                     class="compact",
                     width="100%",
                     options = list(pageLength = 10,
                                    scrollX=T),
                     colnames = c("No. of pupils",
                                  "Avg Scores",
                                  "Std Dev",
                                  "Std Error")) %>%
         formatRound(columns=c('mean', 'sd', 'se'),
                     digits=2))

title: Hands-on Exercise 4c: Visualising Uncertainty
text:
Visualising Uncertainty — ggdist methods

stat_pointinterval()

The plotThe code chunk














exam %>%
  ggplot(aes(x = RACE,
             y = MATHS)) +

       subtitle = "Mean Point + Multiple-interval plot")
