title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text: Ternary plots display the distribution of three-part compositional data. Each side of the triangle represents one of three components. In this exercise I used Singapore population data to explore the proportion of young, economically active and old residents by planning area.

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text: Ternary plots display the distribution of three-part compositional data. Each side of the triangle represents one of three components. In this exercise I used Singapore population data to explore the proportion of young, economically active and old residents by planning area.

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text:
Reflections
A ternary plot is useful when three variables always sum to a fixed total, something a standard scatter plot cannot show. The key takeaway is that the position of each point encodes all three proportions simultaneously, making it easy to spot which planning areas are ageing faster than others. The interactive plotly version added practical value since hovering reveals the exact planning area behind each point.

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(plotly, ggtern, tidyverse)



Importing data

pop_data <- read_csv("respopagsex2000to2018_tidy.csv")



Preparing the data

agpop_mutated <- pop_data %>%
  mutate(`Year` = as.character(Year)) %>%
  spread(AG, Population) %>%
  mutate(YOUNG = rowSums(.[4:8])) %>%
  mutate(ACTIVE = rowSums(.[9:16])) %>%
  mutate(OLD = rowSums(.[17:21])) %>%
  mutate(TOTAL = rowSums(.[22:24])) %>%
  filter(Year == "2018") %>%
  filter(TOTAL > 0)

title: 5a. Building Ternary Plot with R
text:
Plotting Ternary Diagram with R

Static ternary plot — basic

The plotThe code chunk














ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point()






Static ternary plot — with theme

The plotThe code chunk














ggtern(data = agpop_mutated,
       aes(x = YOUNG, y = ACTIVE, z = OLD)) +
  geom_point() +
  labs(title = "Population structure, 2018") +
  theme_rgbw()






Interactive ternary plot using plotly

The plotThe code chunk











label <- function(txt) {
  list(
    text = txt, x = 0.1, y = 1,
    ax = 0, ay = 0,
    xref = "paper", yref = "paper",
    align = "center",
    font = list(family = "serif", size = 15, color = "white"),
    bgcolor = "#b3b3b3", bordercolor = "black", borderwidth = 2
  )
}
axis <- function(txt) {
  list(title = txt, tickformat = ".0%", tickfont = list(size = 10))
}
ternaryAxes <- list(
  aaxis = axis("Young"),
  baxis = axis("Active"),
  caxis = axis("Old")
)
plot_ly(
  agpop_mutated,
  a = ~YOUNG, b = ~ACTIVE, c = ~OLD,
  color = I("black"),
  type = "scatterternary"
) |>
  layout(annotations = label("Ternary Markers"),
         ternary = ternaryAxes)

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text: Heatmaps visualise multivariate data through colour variations, making it easy to spot patterns across many variables and observations at once. In this exercise I used the World Happiness 2018 data.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text: Heatmaps visualise multivariate data through colour variations, making it easy to spot patterns across many variables and observations at once. In this exercise I used the World Happiness 2018 data.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Reflections
The main takeaway is that normalising the data before plotting is essential. Without it, variables with larger absolute values dominate the colour scale and make other variables impossible to compare. The heatmaply interactive version adds real value over the static version because you can hover over individual cells to read exact values and zoom into specific regions. Clustering the rows and columns together also helped reveal groups of countries with similar happiness profiles.

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(seriation, dendextend, heatmaply, tidyverse)



Importing and preparing data

wh <- read_csv("WHData-2018.csv")
row.names(wh) <- wh$Country
wh1 <- dplyr::select(wh, c(3, 7:12))
wh_matrix <- data.matrix(wh)

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Static Heatmap

Basic heatmap — no clustering

The plotThe code chunk



wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)











wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      Rowv = NA, Colv = NA)






Cluster heatmap

The plotThe code chunk



wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix)











wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix)






Normalised heatmap

The plotThe code chunk



wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      scale = "column",
                      cexRow = 0.6,
                      cexCol = 0.8,
                      margins = c(10, 4))











wh_heatmap <- heatmap(wh_matrix,
                      scale = "column",
                      cexRow = 0.6,
                      cexCol = 0.8,
                      margins = c(10, 4))

text:

title: 5c. Visual Multivariate Analysis with Heatmap
text:
Clustering Algorithm

Manual approach

The plotThe code chunk



heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "ward.D")








heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "ward.D")






Statistical approach — finding optimal k

wh_d <- dist(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
             method = "euclidean")
dend_expend(wh_d)[[3]]

  dist_methods hclust_methods     optim
1      unknown         ward.D 0.6137851
2      unknown        ward.D2 0.6289186
3      unknown         single 0.4774362
4      unknown       complete 0.6434009
5      unknown        average 0.6701688
6      unknown       mcquitty 0.5020102
7      unknown         median 0.5901833
8      unknown       centroid 0.6338734



wh_clust <- hclust(wh_d, method = "average")
num_k <- find_k(wh_clust)
plot(num_k)









The plotThe code chunk



heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "average",
          k_row = 3)








heatmaply(normalize(wh_matrix[, -c(1, 2, 4, 5)]),
          dist_method = "euclidean",
          hclust_method = "average",
          k_row = 3)

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text: Treemaps display hierarchical data using nested rectangles where the size of each rectangle represents a quantitative value. In this exercise I used Singapore private property transaction data from 2018.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text: Treemaps display hierarchical data using nested rectangles where the size of each rectangle represents a quantitative value. In this exercise I used Singapore private property transaction data from 2018.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Reflections
Treemaps are effective when you need to show both hierarchy and proportion in one chart. The size encoding shows total units sold at a glance, while the colour encoding adds a second layer of information on median unit price. The treemapify approach using ggplot2 syntax was more intuitive since the layering logic is familiar. The data wrangling step using group_by() and summarise() before plotting was a good reminder that most real-world visualisation work starts with data preparation.

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Getting Started

Loading packages

pacman::p_load(treemap, treemapify, tidyverse)



Importing data

realis2018 <- read_csv("realis2018.csv")

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Data Wrangling

Grouped summaries without the Pipe

realis2018_grouped <- group_by(realis2018, `Project Name`,
                               `Planning Region`, `Planning Area`, 
                               `Property Type`, `Type of Sale`)
realis2018_summarised <- summarise(realis2018_grouped, 
                          `Total Unit Sold` = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Total Area` = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
                          `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE), 
                          `Median Transacted Price` = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))



Grouped summaries with the pipe

realis2018_summarised <- realis2018 %>% 
  group_by(`Project Name`,`Planning Region`, 
           `Planning Area`, `Property Type`, 
           `Type of Sale`) %>%
  summarise(`Total Unit Sold` = sum(`No. of Units`, na.rm = TRUE), 
            `Total Area` = sum(`Area (sqm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Unit Price ($ psm)` = median(`Unit Price ($ psm)`, na.rm = TRUE),
            `Median Transacted Price` = median(`Transacted Price ($)`, na.rm = TRUE))



Filtering resale condominiums

realis2018_selected <- realis2018_summarised %>%
  filter(`Property Type` == "Condominium", `Type of Sale` == "Resale")

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Designing Treemap with treemap Package

Designing a static treemap

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )






Working with vColor and type arguments

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type = "value",
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )






The “value” type treemap

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type="value",
        palette="RdYlBu", 
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )






The “manual” type treemap

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type="manual",
        palette="RdYlBu", 
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )




The colour scheme above is confusing because RdYlBu is a diverging palette but all values are positive. A single-colour palette like Blues is more appropriate.

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type="manual",
        palette="Blues", 
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )






Working with algorithm argument

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type="manual",
        palette="Blues",
        algorithm = "squarified",
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )






Using sortID

The plotThe code chunk














treemap(realis2018_selected,
        index=c("Planning Region", "Planning Area", "Project Name"),
        vSize="Total Unit Sold",
        vColor="Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
        type="manual",
        palette="Blues",
        algorithm = "pivotSize",
        sortID = "Median Transacted Price",
        title="Resale Condominium by Planning Region and Area, 2017",
        title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
        )

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Designing Treemap using treemapify Package

Designing a basic treemap

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(data=realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`),
       layout = "scol",
       start = "bottomleft") +
  geom_treemap() +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light blue", high = "blue")






Defining hierarchy
Group by Planning Region

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(data=realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`),
       start = "topleft") +
  geom_treemap()




Group by Planning Area

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(data=realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) +
  geom_treemap()




Adding boundary lines

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(data=realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`,
           subgroup2 = `Planning Area`)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup2_border(colour = "gray40",
                                size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "gray20")






With hierarchy and labels

The plotThe code chunk














ggplot(data = realis2018_selected,
       aes(area = `Total Unit Sold`,
           fill = `Median Unit Price ($ psm)`,
           label = `Project Name`,
           subgroup = `Planning Region`)) +
  geom_treemap() +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_border(colour = "grey40", size = 2) +
  geom_treemap_subgroup_text(place = "centre",
                             grow = FALSE,
                             alpha = 0.5,
                             colour = "black",
                             fontface = "italic",
                             min.size = 0) +
  geom_treemap_text(colour = "white",
                    place = "topleft",
                    reflow = TRUE) +
  scale_fill_gradient(low = "light yellow", high = "red") +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

title: 5e. Treemap Visualisation with R
text:
Designing Interactive Treemap using d3treeR

Installing d3treeR

install.packages("devtools")
install.packages("treemap")

library(devtools)
install_github("timelyportfolio/d3treeR")



Loading packages

library(treemap)
library(d3treeR)



Designing an interactive treemap

tm <- treemap(
  realis2018_summarised,
  index = c("Planning Region", "Planning Area"),
  vSize = "Total Unit Sold",
  vColor = "Median Unit Price ($ psm)",
  type = "value",
  title = "Private Residential Property Sold, 2017",
  title.legend = "Median Unit Price (S$ per sq. m)"
)









d3tree(tm, rootname = "Singapore")

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
In-Class Exercise 3 builds on previous Tableau exercises by creating two separate dashboards using different datasets.
The first dashboard uses the Superstore dataset to analyse sales and profit performance across product sub-categories and customers. The second uses SingStat’s June 2025 population data to visualise the age and gender distribution of Singapore residents across three planning areas.

title: In-class Exercise 3
text:
Dashboard 1: SuperStore Sales Report
The interactive dashboard can be viewed on Tableau Public at the link below:
👉 View Dashboard on Tableau Public



SuperStore Sales Report


This dashboard contains 5 views:

Sales versus Profit Quadrant by State — a bubble scatter plot mapping each state by sales and profit percentile. Bubble size encodes sales volume and colour encodes profit (blue = profitable, orange = loss-making).
SuperStore Sales Report — a general overview of overall sales performance across the dataset.
SubCategory and Profit — a Pareto chart ranking sub-categories by profit, with a cumulative line showing that Copiers, Phones, and Accessories drive the majority of total profit.
Customer ID and Profit — customers ranked by individual profit contribution, with a running cumulative sum and average reference line.
Pareto Chart by Customer ID — The x-axis represents the cumulative percentage of customers, while the y-axis represents the cumulative percentage of total profit. The two dashed reference lines intersect at the 20% customer mark and 80% profit mark, confirming the Pareto principle. The curve rises steeply in the early portion before gradually levelling off, indicating that a small segment of customers generates a disproportionately large share of profit.




SubCategory and Profit — Pareto Chart





Customer ID and Profit





Pareto Chart by Customer ID

title: In-class Exercise 3
section: Dashboard 2: Distribution by Age and Region
text:
Dashboard 2: Distribution by Age and Region
The interactive dashboard can be viewed on Tableau Public at the link below:
👉 View Dashboard on Tableau Public
Data source: respopagesexfa2025.csv from SingStat — Singapore Residents by Planning Area/Subzone, Age Group, Sex and Floor Area of Residence, June 2025.



Distribution by Age and Region — Ang Mo Kio, Tampines, Punggol


The side-by-side population pyramids compare three planning areas:

Ang Mo Kio has a concentration in the middle-to-older age bands (40–74), reflecting a mature, established residential town.
Tampines shows a broad working-age base (30–69), consistent with a large, well-developed HDB estate.
Punggol has a distinctly younger profile — the largest bars sit in the 25–44 range with a strong base of young children, reflecting its newer development attracting young families.

Pink bars represent Females and blue bars represent Males.
Pareto Charts were a key new technique in this exercise. By combining a sorted bar chart with a cumulative percentage line, I could quickly identify which sub-categories and customers drive the most profit. The 80/20 pattern appeared clearly in both the sub-category and customer-level analyses.
Population Pyramids required a different approach — splitting the dataset by sex and mirroring bars on opposite sides of a central axis. Comparing three planning areas side-by-side made demographic differences immediately visible, something a table of numbers could not achieve as effectively.

title: In-Class Exercise 5: Treemap Visualisation with Tableau
section: Dashboard 1 — Treemap of Number of Units Sold and Unit Price by Geography
text:
Dashboard 1 — Treemap of Number of Units Sold and Unit Price by Geography
This treemap visualises the distribution of private residential property units sold across Singapore planning regions and areas. The size of each rectangle represents the number of units sold, while the colour intensity shows the average unit price in dollars per square foot.



Treemap of Number of Units Sold and Unit Price by Geography


From this treemap, it is clear that the Central Region dominates in terms of total units sold, taking up the largest proportion of the treemap. Within the Central Region, areas like Bukit Timah and Novena stand out. The colour gradient also reveals that some smaller areas command significantly higher unit prices despite having fewer transactions.

title: In-Class Exercise 5: Treemap Visualisation with Tableau
section: Dashboard 2 — Treemap Sales and Profit by Geography
text:
Dashboard 2 — Treemap Sales and Profit by Geography
This treemap visualises Superstore sales and profit across different US regions and states. The size of each rectangle represents sales volume, while the colour encodes profit, blue indicates positive profit and orange/yellow indicates losses or lower profit.



Treemap Sales and Profit by Geography


The dashboard also includes an interactive filter by year (2022–2025) and a tooltip showing detailed city-level profit and sales trends over time. From the treemap, California in the West region has the highest sales volume, while some Central states like Texas show relatively lower profitability despite sizeable sales.
